[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation Hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}
{{温泉}}

=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

{{温泉}}
[[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Radiation]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:放射能泉]]

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

revision.text:
[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}

Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>

For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}

Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.

At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}

[[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Radiation]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:放射能泉]]

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

[[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Radiation]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Radioactive springs-qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Radioactive springs-qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Radioactive springs-qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]] [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:放射能泉]]

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

[[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Radiation]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Radioactive springs-qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Radioactive springs-qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Radioactive springs-qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:放射能泉]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Radioactive springs]][[Category:11]]

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}
