Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}}
{{温泉}}

== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or qq1 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}

[[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Radiation]] <!--Radioactive springs-->[[Category:Radioactive springs]]<!--Radioactive springs-->[[Category:Radioactive springs]]<!--Radioactive springs-->[[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:放射能泉]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:adioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]]

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or qq1 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}} * [[:en:Radium_Springs|Radium Springs]]
{{温泉}}

However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or qq1 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}} * [[:en:Radium_Springs|Radium Springs]]
{{温泉}}

[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質|1=Hot Spring Types}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>

; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or qq1 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />

For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症|1=General Indications for hot spring use}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.

=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症}} apply.

== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症|1=General Indications for hot spring use}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症|1=General_Indications_for_hot_spring_use#General_Contraindications|lt=General Contraindications for hot spring use}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}} * [[:en:Radium_Springs|Radium Springs]]
{{温泉}}
[[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Radiation]] <!--Radioactive springs-->[[Category:Radioactive springs]]<!--Radioactive springs-->[[Category:Radioactive springs]]<!--Radioactive springs-->[[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Radioactive springs]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Radioactive springs]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:放射能泉]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:adioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Radioactive springs]][[Category:qq]] {{Onsen}}

{{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}, known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Onsen Water Quality|ja|温泉の泉質|1=Hot Spring Types}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name=JBSJ /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name=JBSJ />
Radioactive springs are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref>
== Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }} | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }} |}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or qq1 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }}</ref> ; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}} Radioactive springs contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}}, {{ill|radon|en|Radon}}, {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}}, and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}}. For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}, drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症|1=General Indications for hot spring use}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}}, {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}}, chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}}, chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}}, and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}}.
==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}}, and joint pain.
=== Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症|1=General_Indications_for_hot_spring_use#General_Contraindications|lt=General Contraindications for hot spring use}} apply.
== Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}} (Tottori) * {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} (Hyogo) * {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} (Yamanashi) * {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}} (Akita) * Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}} (Fukushima) * {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}} (Fukushima) * Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}} – Australia * {{ill|Ramsar, Mazandaran|en|Ramsar}} – Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name=ra>{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }}</ref> * {{ill|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Radiation hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} * {{ill|Natural radiation|en|Natural radiation}} * {{ill|Environmental radiation|en|Environmental radiation}} * {{ill|Natural radioactivity|en|Natural radioactivity}} * [[:en:Radium_Springs|Radium Springs]]

{{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} , known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name="JBSJ" /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name="JBSJ" />
Radioactive spring are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref> == Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }}
| {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }}
|}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name="JBSJ">{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }} </ref>
; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} , with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|:en:Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}}
Radioactive spring contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}} , {{ill|radon|en|Radon}} , {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}} , and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}} . For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} , drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症|1=General Indications for hot spring use}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}} , {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}} , {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}} , chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}} , chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}} , chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}} , and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}} . ==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}} , and joint pain. === Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症|1=General_Indications_for_hot_spring_use#General_Contraindications|lt=General Contraindications for hot spring use}} apply. == Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}(Tottori)
* {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}}(Tottori)

{{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|Kawaraburo at {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} , known for its radon spring]] '''Radioactive Spring''' (放射能泉, Hōshanōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}} based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. According to the {{ill|Onsen Act|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}, it is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to the presence of special components.<ref name="JBSJ" /> Approximately 8% of hot springs in Japan fall under this category.<ref name="JBSJ" />
Radioactive spring are often collectively referred to as "radium springs" (ラジウム泉), regardless of their radium content. However, in Japan, most such springs are rich in radon and are commonly called {{ill|Radon Hot Springs|ja|ラドン温泉}} (ラドン泉). Springs containing high levels of radon's isotope, {{ill|Thoron|ja|ラドン220}}, are also referred to as "Thoron Hot Springs" (トロン泉).<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm The Science of Hot Springs: About Radioactive Springs] (関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん)</ref> == Definition == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ Comparison of Old and New Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! scope="col" | Old Label ! scope="col" | New Label ! scope="col" | Abbreviated Label |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Radioactive Spring * Simple Radioactive Spring * Weak Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) * Radioactive Spring with Components (e.g., Na, Cl) }}
| {{Plainlist| * Simple Weak Rn Spring * Simple Rn Spring * Weak Rn-Component Spring * Rn-Component Spring }}
|}
; Radioactive Spring : Contains at least 3 nanocuries of radon per kilogram of water (equivalent to 8.25 {{ill|Mache units|en|Mache unit}} or 111 {{ill|Becquerel|en|Bq}}).<ref name="JBSJ">{{Cite journal|title=Radioactivity and Hot Springs: Onsen Science |author=Masayoshi Yamamoto, Junpei Tomita |journal=Onsen Science |publisher=Japan Society of Hot Spring Science |volume=64 |issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241 |year=2015 }} </ref>
; Weak Radioactive Spring : Contains 8.25–50 Mache units of radon per kilogram. ; Radioactive Spring : Contains 50 Mache units or more of radon per kilogram.
If these criteria are met, the onsen is categorized as a radioactive spring, and many springs are labeled as “含放射能○○泉” or “含弱放射能□□泉” (e.g., “Weak Rn-Na-Cl Spring”). The highest recorded level in Japan is at the Tenmangu Spring in {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} , with 14 Bq/L.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Usage == {{main|:en:Radiation hormesis#Environmental Use of Radioactive Springs}}
Radioactive spring contain trace amounts of {{ill|radium|en|Radium}} , {{ill|radon|en|Radon}} , {{ill|astatine|en|Astatine}} , and other radioactive isotopes from {{ill|nuclear decay|en|Decay (radioactivity)}} . For instance, near the source at Arima Onsen, exposure levels reach 13 μSv/h, which is comparable to levels requiring indoor sheltering under Japan's nuclear safety guidelines.
Proponents claim no adverse health effects and argue for {{ill|hormesis|en|Radiation hormesis}} effects, asserting that radiation activates immune cells and promotes health. Critics point out potential health risks from radon exposure. Thus, radioactive springs and the hormesis theory remain subjects of both support and skepticism.
One study estimated a radiation dose of 2.8 nSv from a 2-hour bath in water containing 3.9 Bq/kg of radon. Adding 500 ml of drinking water from the spring increases the dose to 5.1 nSv. At {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}} , drinking 2.2 L/day of water with 617 mBq/L radon results in an annual dose of 0.14 mSv.<ref name=JBSJ />
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * {{ill|General Indications for hot spring use|lt=General indications|ja|一般的適応症|1=General Indications for hot spring use}}, {{ill|Gout|en|Gout}} , {{ill|Hypertension|en|High blood pressure}} , {{ill|Atherosclerosis|en|Atherosclerosis}} , chronic {{ill|Skin disease|en|Skin disease}} , chronic {{ill|Gynecological diseases|en|Gynecology}} , chronic {{ill|Cholecystitis|en|Cholecystitis}} , and {{ill|Cholelithiasis|en|Gallstone}} . ==== Drinking ==== * Gout, chronic gastrointestinal diseases, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones, {{ill|Neuralgia|en|Neuralgia}}, {{ill|Muscle pain|en|Myalgia}} , and joint pain. === Contraindications === * General contraindications for {{ill|Hot springs|ja|温泉#一般的禁忌症|1=General_Indications_for_hot_spring_use#General_Contraindications|lt=General Contraindications for hot spring use}} apply. == Notable Hot Springs == === In Japan === * Futamata Radium Onsen (Hokkaido) * {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}}(Tottori)
* {{ill|Sekigane Onsen|ja|関金温泉}}(Tottori)
* {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}(Hyogo)
* {{ill|Masutomi Onsen|ja|増富温泉}}(Yamanashi)
* {{ill|Tamagawa Onsen (Akita)|ja|玉川温泉 (秋田県)}}(Akita)
* Ena Gorge Onsen (Gifu) * Ikeda Radium Onsen (Shimane) * {{ill|Bobata Onsen|ja|母畑温泉}}(Fukushima)
* {{ill|Nekodaki Onsen|ja|猫啼温泉}}(Fukushima)
* Baba-no-Yu Onsen (Fukushima)
=== Outside Japan === * {{ill|Arkaroola|en|Arkaroola}}– Australia
* [[:en:Ramsar,_Iran|Ramsar, Iran]]– Known for its radium-rich springs and high natural radiation.<ref name="ra">{{Cite journal|title=Exposure to 226Ra from Vegetable Consumption in Ramsar |url=https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X }} </ref>

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|ラドン泉として知られる[[三朝温泉]]の河原風呂]] '''放射能泉'''（ほうしゃのうせん, Radioactive Spring）は、掲示用泉質名に基づく[[温泉]]の[[泉質]]の分類の一種。[[温泉法]]では特殊成分を含む[[温泉#療養泉|療養泉]]（medical springs）に分類される<ref name=JBSJ/>。日本の温泉において、8%ほどが放射能泉に該当する<ref name=JBSJ />。
放射能泉はラジウムの含有量に関係なく、'''ラジウム温泉'''（ラジウム泉）と総称されることもあるが、日本の場合はラドン含有量の多いものがほとんどであり、そのような温泉は一般的に「'''[[ラドン#ラドン温泉|ラドン温泉]]'''」（ラドン泉）と呼ばれている<ref>[http://kadoyasan.com/radium-world.html 日本全国ラジウム温泉ラドン温泉一覧表/効能/日本四大ラジウム温泉]（新潟県五頭温泉郷村杉温泉 角屋旅館）</ref>。この他、[[ラドンの同位体]]である[[ラドン220|トロン]]を多く含むものもあり、同様に「'''トロン温泉'''」（トロン泉）と呼ばれている<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm 温泉の科学：放射能泉について]（関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん）</ref>。
== 泉質の定義 == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ 新旧泉質名の対応<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=参考資料２新旧泉質名対照 |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=環境省自然環境局自然環境整備課温泉地保護利用推進室 |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" id="旧泉質名" | 旧泉質名 ! scope="col" id="新泉質名" | 新泉質名 ! scope="col" id="略記泉質名" | 略記泉質名 |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * 単純弱放射能泉 * 単純放射能泉 * 含弱放射能－○－○泉 * または含放射能－○－○泉 }} | {{Plainlist| * 単純弱Rn泉 * 単純Rn泉 * 含弱Rn－○－○泉 * 含Rn－○－○泉 }} |}
; 放射能泉 : 温泉水1kg中にラドンを3[[ナノ]][[キュリー]]（ = 8.25[[マッヘ単位]] = 111[[ベクレル|Bq]]）以上含有<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|和書|author=山本政儀 |author2=富田純平 |date=2015 |journal=放射能と温泉. 温泉科学 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |volume=64|issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241}}</ref>。 ; 単純弱放射能泉・含弱放射能泉 : ラドン含有量は 8.25マッヘ単位/kg 以上、50マッヘ単位/kg 未満 ; 単純放射能泉・含放射能泉 : 50マッヘ単位/kg 以上。
放射能泉の場合、前述の泉質定義に合致すれば放射能泉となる。そのため、新泉質名による分類では“含放射能○○泉”、または“含弱放射能□□泉”と付く多くの泉質が存在する。
国内の最高値は、[[有馬温泉]]天満宮の湯において 14Bq/Lであった<ref name=JBSJ />。
== 利用 == {{Main|放射線ホルミシス#環境放射線の積極的な利用としての放射能泉}} 微量の[[ラジウム]]、[[ラドン]]および[[アスタチン]]から[[水銀]]までの[[原子核崩壊]]によって生じる、[[放射性同位体]]が含まれているのが特徴である。たとえば[[有馬温泉]]の源泉近くでは、13[[マイクロ]][[シーベルト]]/時を[[被曝]]する。この量は、[[原子力安全委員会]]の指針では屋内退避が推奨される被曝量である。
これに対し、「健康への悪影響は一切なくむしろ[[ホルミシス効果]]による[[免疫]]細胞の活性化により健康になるため積極的に被曝すべき」とする者もいる。その一方で、ラドン被曝などでの健康に対する害への指摘もある。このように放射能泉やホルミシス効果には、益とする意見と害とする意見があり、賛否両論である。
ある研究では、3.9Bq/kgの温泉に2時間入浴した場合の被ばく量は 2.8nSv、加えて500mlを飲用すると合計5.1nSvであるとされる<ref name=JBSJ />。[[増富温泉]](617mBq/L)を毎日2.2L飲用した場合では、年間線量は0.14mSVと試算される<ref name=JBSJ />。
== 効能 == ''※[[効能]]はその効果を万人に保証するものではない''
泉質に基づく効能として、以下が挙げられる<ref>[http://www.pref.aichi.jp/eiseiken/4f/kinteki.html 環境庁自然保護局長通知（昭和57年環自施第227号）]、[http://www.spa.or.jp/hyakkka/7.htm 適応症と禁忌症（社団法人日本温泉協会）]</ref>。
=== 適応症 === ==== 浴用 ==== * [[一般的適応症]]のほか、[[痛風]]、[[高血圧|高血圧症]]、[[動脈硬化症]]、慢性[[皮膚病]]、慢性[[婦人病]]、慢性[[胆嚢炎]]、[[胆石症]]。
==== 飲用 ==== * 痛風、慢性[[消化器]]病、慢性胆嚢炎、胆石症、[[神経痛]]、[[筋肉痛]]、[[関節]]痛。
=== 禁忌症 === * 浴用において[[一般的適応症#一般的禁忌症|一般的禁忌症]]。
== 代表的な温泉地 == === 日本国内 === * 二股ラジウム温泉（北海道） * [[三朝温泉]]（鳥取県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * [[関金温泉]]（鳥取県） * [[有馬温泉]]（兵庫県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * [[増富温泉]]（山梨県）<ref name=JBSJ/>, 国内最高 * [[玉川温泉 (秋田県)|玉川温泉]]（秋田県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * 恵那峡温泉（岐阜県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * 池田ラジウム鉱泉（島根県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * [[母畑温泉]]（福島県） * [[猫啼温泉]]（福島県） * 馬場の湯温泉（福島県）
=== 日本以外 === * {{ill2|アーカルーラ|en|Arkaroola}} ‐オーストラリアにある放射能泉がある地域。 * [[ラームサル]] ‐ラジウムを含む温泉が湧くため、高い放射線が確認される。国際的に高い[[環境放射線]]の地域とされており、影響と健康の研究が行われている<ref name=ra>[https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X Exposure to 226Ra from consumption of vegetables in the high level natural radiation area of Ramsar-Iran]</ref>。 * {{ill2|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[放射線ホルミシス]] * [[自然放射線]] * [[環境放射線]] * [[自然放射能]]
{{温泉}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ほうしやのうせん}} [[Category:温泉の泉質]] [[Category:放射線]]

[[ファイル:Misasa onsen02n4592.jpg|thumb|right|ラドン泉として知られる[[三朝温泉]]の河原風呂]] '''放射能泉'''（ほうしゃのうせん, Radioactive Spring）は、掲示用泉質名に基づく[[温泉]]の[[泉質]]の分類の一種。[[温泉法]]では特殊成分を含む[[温泉#療養泉|療養泉]]（medical springs）に分類される<ref name=JBSJ/>。日本の温泉において、8%ほどが放射能泉に該当する<ref name=JBSJ />。
放射能泉はラジウムの含有量に関係なく、'''ラジウム温泉'''（ラジウム泉）と総称されることもあるが、日本の場合はラドン含有量の多いものがほとんどであり、そのような温泉は一般的に「'''[[ラドン#ラドン温泉|ラドン温泉]]'''」（ラドン泉）と呼ばれている<ref>[http://kadoyasan.com/radium-world.html 日本全国ラジウム温泉ラドン温泉一覧表/効能/日本四大ラジウム温泉]（新潟県五頭温泉郷村杉温泉 角屋旅館）</ref>。この他、[[ラドンの同位体]]である[[ラドン220|トロン]]を多く含むものもあり、同様に「'''トロン温泉'''」（トロン泉）と呼ばれている<ref>[https://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~ue3t-cb/bbs/special/sience_of_hotspring/sience_of_hotspring_4-6.htm 温泉の科学：放射能泉について]（関東周辺立ち寄り温泉みしゅらん）</ref>。
== 泉質の定義 == {| class="wikitable floatright" style="margin:1em; font-size:95%" |+ 新旧泉質名の対応<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=参考資料２新旧泉質名対照 |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=環境省自然環境局自然環境整備課温泉地保護利用推進室 |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" id="旧泉質名" | 旧泉質名 ! scope="col" id="新泉質名" | 新泉質名 ! scope="col" id="略記泉質名" | 略記泉質名 |- style="background-color:#eef" | 放射能泉 | {{Plainlist| * 単純弱放射能泉 * 単純放射能泉 * 含弱放射能－○－○泉 * または含放射能－○－○泉 }} | {{Plainlist| * 単純弱Rn泉 * 単純Rn泉 * 含弱Rn－○－○泉 * 含Rn－○－○泉 }} |}
; 放射能泉 : 温泉水1kg中にラドンを3[[ナノ]][[キュリー]]（ = 8.25[[マッヘ単位]] = 111[[ベクレル|Bq]]）以上含有<ref name=JBSJ>{{Cite journal|和書|author=山本政儀 |author2=富田純平 |date=2015 |journal=放射能と温泉. 温泉科学 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |volume=64|issue=4 |pages=388-401 |naid=40020464241}}</ref>。 ; 単純弱放射能泉・含弱放射能泉 : ラドン含有量は 8.25マッヘ単位/kg 以上、50マッヘ単位/kg 未満 ; 単純放射能泉・含放射能泉 : 50マッヘ単位/kg 以上。
放射能泉の場合、前述の泉質定義に合致すれば放射能泉となる。そのため、新泉質名による分類では“含放射能○○泉”、または“含弱放射能□□泉”と付く多くの泉質が存在する。
国内の最高値は、[[有馬温泉]]天満宮の湯において 14Bq/Lであった<ref name=JBSJ />。
== 利用 == {{Main|放射線ホルミシス#環境放射線の積極的な利用としての放射能泉}} 微量の[[ラジウム]]、[[ラドン]]および[[アスタチン]]から[[水銀]]までの[[原子核崩壊]]によって生じる、[[放射性同位体]]が含まれているのが特徴である。たとえば[[有馬温泉]]の源泉近くでは、13[[マイクロ]][[シーベルト]]/時を[[被曝]]する。この量は、[[原子力安全委員会]]の指針では屋内退避が推奨される被曝量である。
これに対し、「健康への悪影響は一切なくむしろ[[ホルミシス効果]]による[[免疫]]細胞の活性化により健康になるため積極的に被曝すべき」とする者もいる。その一方で、ラドン被曝などでの健康に対する害への指摘もある。このように放射能泉やホルミシス効果には、益とする意見と害とする意見があり、賛否両論である。
ある研究では、3.9Bq/kgの温泉に2時間入浴した場合の被ばく量は 2.8nSv、加えて500mlを飲用すると合計5.1nSvであるとされる<ref name=JBSJ />。[[増富温泉]](617mBq/L)を毎日2.2L飲用した場合では、年間線量は0.14mSVと試算される<ref name=JBSJ />。
== 効能 == ''※[[効能]]はその効果を万人に保証するものではない''
泉質に基づく効能として、以下が挙げられる<ref>[http://www.pref.aichi.jp/eiseiken/4f/kinteki.html 環境庁自然保護局長通知（昭和57年環自施第227号）]、[http://www.spa.or.jp/hyakkka/7.htm 適応症と禁忌症（社団法人日本温泉協会）]</ref>。
=== 適応症 === ==== 浴用 ==== * [[一般的適応症]]のほか、[[痛風]]、[[高血圧|高血圧症]]、[[動脈硬化症]]、慢性[[皮膚病]]、慢性[[婦人病]]、慢性[[胆嚢炎]]、[[胆石症]]。
==== 飲用 ==== * 痛風、慢性[[消化器]]病、慢性胆嚢炎、胆石症、[[神経痛]]、[[筋肉痛]]、[[関節]]痛。
=== 禁忌症 === * 浴用において[[一般的適応症#一般的禁忌症|一般的禁忌症]]。
== 代表的な温泉地 == === 日本国内 === * 二股ラジウム温泉（北海道） * [[三朝温泉]]（鳥取県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * [[関金温泉]]（鳥取県） * [[有馬温泉]]（兵庫県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * [[増富温泉]]（山梨県）<ref name=JBSJ/>, 国内最高 * [[玉川温泉 (秋田県)|玉川温泉]]（秋田県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * 恵那峡温泉（岐阜県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * 池田ラジウム鉱泉（島根県）<ref name=JBSJ/> * [[母畑温泉]]（福島県） * [[猫啼温泉]]（福島県） * 馬場の湯温泉（福島県）
=== 日本以外 === * {{ill2|アーカルーラ|en|Arkaroola}} ‐オーストラリアにある放射能泉がある地域。 * [[ラームサル]] ‐ラジウムを含む温泉が湧くため、高い放射線が確認される。国際的に高い[[環境放射線]]の地域とされており、影響と健康の研究が行われている<ref name=ra>[https://doi.org/10.1016/S0265-931X(02)00108-X Exposure to 226Ra from consumption of vegetables in the high level natural radiation area of Ramsar-Iran]</ref>。 * {{ill2|Radium Hot Springs|en|Radium Hot Springs}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[放射線ホルミシス]] * [[自然放射線]] * [[環境放射線]] * [[自然放射能]]
{{温泉}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ほうしやのうせん}} [[Category:温泉の泉質]] [[Category:放射線]]
